title: Creating a waterfall edge on a foam seat cushion
subtitle: Cut and profile foam so the top surface curves continuously into the front face without a visible corner
excerpt: How to achieve a waterfall edge on an upholstered seat cushion by carving a bevel on the foam front corner and wrapping the whole cushion with Dacron wadding to smooth the transition, so that no hard right-angle edge is visible through the cover fabric.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture Upholstery. BFTA cushion construction guidance. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cushion or seat edge profile in which the top surface flows continuously into the front face without a visible right-angle corner. Achieved by carving a 45-degree bevel on the foam corner, or by gluing a separate wedge of softer foam to the corner, then wrapping the whole cushion in Dacron. The Dacron layer fills the bevel and creates a smooth curve through the cover fabric. The effect reads as a rounded, generous front edge rather than a sharp-cornered block.

content.text: is the standard profile on quality seat cushions. A right-angle foam corner produces a visible line through woven fabric, however well it is covered. The technique needs only a utility knife and Dacron. The bevel angle sets how soft the edge looks: a shallow bevel (30 degrees) gives a gentle softening; a steep bevel (60 degrees) gives a clearly rounded front profile.

Foam cushion block cut to the finished seat size and depth. Dacron wadding, 100 g/m² or heavier, cut 100 mm larger than the cushion all around. Foam adhesive spray for bonding the bevel if using a separate wedge approach.
Step 1, mark the bevel on the foam front corner

On the front face of the foam block, mark a line 30 mm down from the top edge. On the top face, mark a line 30 mm back from the front edge. These two marks define the corners of a triangular section to be removed. Using the utility knife, cut from the top-face mark down to the front-face mark, angling the blade to create a clean 45-degree bevel. Remove the triangular offcut. The front corner of the foam block now has a flat angled face.
Step 2, round off the bevel with Dacron

Lay the foam block on the cut sheet of Dacron, top-face down. Wrap the Dacron over the front face and bevel, pulling it gently around the angled corner without compressing the foam. The Dacron fills the flat bevel surface and creates the impression of a smooth curve from top to front. Trim the Dacron 20 mm past the bevel so the wrap terminates on the underside of the cushion, not on the front face. Hold the Dacron in position with a few staples into the foam underside, or rely on the cover fabric tension to hold it in the finished cushion.
Step 3, cut and fit the cover fabric

Cut the seat cushion cover fabric to allow for the full depth of the front face plus the bevel length when measuring from top to bottom. The front panel of the cover should include the top surface, bevel area, and front face in a single continuous piece, not a separate top and front section joined at the corner. Sew the front panel to the side gussets and the back panel. Insert the foam and Dacron assembly. Close the back with a zip or slip-stitch.

attrs.symptom: Bevel corner still shows as a ridge through the cover fabric
attrs.cause: The Dacron layer is too thin or was not wrapped fully around the bevel face.
attrs.fix: Remove the cover, add a second layer of Dacron over the bevel only, and re-insert. Two layers of 100 g/m² Dacron at the bevel is usually sufficient to eliminate the ridge.

attrs.symptom: The front face of the cover fabric pulls inward slightly under the bevel
attrs.cause: The front panel of the cover was cut too short and is being pulled upward by the foam's attempt to fill the cover.
attrs.fix: Re-cut the front panel with an additional 20 mm depth to ensure it reaches the full front face of the foam below the bevel without pulling.
